title: Brain implant helps paralysed man to feed himself and drink from cup
content: # Brain Implant Helps Paralysed Man to Feed Himself and Drink from Cup **LOPINUZE — In a landmark achievement for neural engineering, a 45-year-old man paralysed from the chest down in a swimming accident six years ago has regained the ability to feed himself and drink from a cup, thanks to an experimental brain implant known as a “double neural bypass.”** Keith Thomas of Massapequa, New York, could not lift his arms off his wheelchair when he agreed to trial the technology in 2021. After surgery to implant electrodes in his brain and months of intensive training, Thomas can now move his arms and hands—and, crucially, feel the sensation of touch again. The breakthrough, published in the journal *Science Translational Medicine*, marks a significant step forward for brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) in restoring both motor function and sensory feedback. “When I first saw my hand move on command, I cried. It was the first time in years I felt hope,” Thomas said in an exclusive interview with LOPINUZE. “Now I can pick up a fork, bring food to my mouth, and even taste my coffee without spilling it. That’s a kind of freedom I thought I’d lost forever.” ## How the ‘Double Neural Bypass’ Works The implant, developed by a team at the Feinstein Institutes for Medical Research in New York, consists of two sets of microelectrode arrays placed in the brain’s motor cortex and somatosensory cortex. These regions control movement and process touch, respectively. The electrodes record neural signals from the motor cortex and transmit them wirelessly to a computer, which decodes the intended movement and sends commands to a custom arm-and-hand brace worn by the patient. Simultaneously, sensors in the brace detect pressure and texture, sending signals back to the somatosensory cortex to recreate the sensation of touch. This closed-loop system—termed a “double neural bypass”—enables Thomas to not only move his limbs but also feel what he is holding. “The sensory feedback is the game-changer,” said Dr. Chad Bouton, principal investigator of the study and vice president of advanced engineering at the Feinstein Institutes. “Without it, patients cannot gauge how hard to grip a cup or how much force to use when lifting a spoon. Keith’s ability to feel temperature, pressure, and texture is what allows him to perform daily tasks independently.” ## Clinical Trial Data and Recovery Timeline According to the study, Thomas underwent a 15-hour surgery in November 2021 to implant the electrodes. Over the following nine months, he participated in 30 training sessions, each lasting three to four hours. By the 12th session, he could lift his arm off the wheelchair. By the 20th session, he was grasping objects. By the 30th session, he was feeding himself. The trial included three other participants with similar injuries, though Thomas showed the most dramatic improvement. Researchers attribute this to his high motivation and the personalized machine-learning algorithms that adapted to his neural signals over time. “What we are seeing is a proof-of-concept that the brain can rewire itself even years after paralysis,” added Dr. Ashesh Mehta, neurosurgeon at Northwell Health and co-author of the study. “This is not just about movement; it’s about restoring dignity and quality of life.” ## Implications for Future Treatments The success of the double neural bypass opens the door to broader applications, including helping patients with spinal cord injuries, stroke-related paralysis, and even neurodegenerative diseases like ALS. However, researchers caution that the technology remains experimental, with risks of infection, device failure, and the need for long-term maintenance. “We are at the very beginning of a new frontier,” said Dr. Bouton. “But Keith’s story shows us what is possible when we combine neurosurgery, artificial intelligence, and relentless human determination.” ## Forward-Looking Analysis As the field of neural implants accelerates, the next decade will likely see more compact, wireless devices that can be implanted with less invasive surgery. Regulatory approval from the FDA for widespread clinical use remains years away, but the momentum is undeniable. For patients like Keith Thomas, the future holds not just the hope of movement, but the promise of independence. **For more on this story and other breakthroughs in Science, stay with LOPINUZE.**
